challenge: The Bible has been corrupted (Tahrif)
one_liner: Over 5,800 Greek manuscripts — many predating Islam — show no systematic corruption. The Quran itself told Muhammad to consult the scriptures.
points: The New Testament manuscript tradition is unmatched in antiquity: 5,800+ Greek manuscripts, 10,000+ Latin, earliest fragments within decades of the originals.
Textual variants across manuscripts are overwhelmingly minor (spelling, word order) — no doctrine is altered by any variant.

The Quran affirms the Torah and Gospel as valid revelation and instructs Muhammad to consult People of the Book if in doubt (Surah 5:47, 10:94). If those texts were already corrupted, this instruction is incoherent.
The Dead Sea Scrolls (discovered 1947, predating Christ by 200–300 years) show Isaiah and the Psalms substantially identical to the text used today — ruling out post-Christian interpolation.
No manuscript tradition anywhere — across Egypt, Syria, Rome, Antioch — shows evidence of coordinated systematic alteration. Tahrif requires a conspiracy with zero surviving trace.
anchor_verse: Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.

challenge: Jesus was not crucified — it only appeared so (Surah 4:157)
one_liner: The crucifixion is one of the most historically secure facts of antiquity, confirmed by hostile non-Christian sources with no motive to support Christian claims.
points: Tacitus (Annals 15.44, c. 116 AD) records that Christ was executed by Pontius Pilate under Tiberius — written by a Roman hostile to Christianity.
Josephus (Antiquities 18.3.3) references the crucifixion. The Babylonian Talmud confirms Jesus was 'hanged on Passover Eve.'
Paul's creed in 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 is dated by critical scholars to within 2–5 years of the crucifixion, naming living eyewitnesses.
The Quran's denial was written 600 years after the event with no cited historical sources — it is a theological assertion, not a historical finding.
If God made it appear Jesus was crucified when he wasn't, God deceived the entire early church — raising serious questions about the character of a God Islam claims is perfectly truthful.
anchor_verse: He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree.

challenge: The Trinity makes Christians polytheists — you worship three gods
one_liner: The Trinity is one God in three Persons — not three gods. Islam's own Quran misidentifies the Trinity as Father, Jesus, and Mary, attacking a position no Christian council ever held.
points: Christianity is explicitly and emphatically monotheist. The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) — 'The Lord our God, the Lord is one' — is foundational to Christian theology.
The Trinity is one divine essence (ousia) subsisting in three distinct Persons (hypostases) — a distinction the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) made precise against all forms of tritheism.

The Quran's version of the Trinity (Surah 5:116) presents Father, Jesus, and Mary — a position no Christian confession, council, or catechism has ever affirmed. Islam is refuting a heresy it invented.

The deeper question Islam cannot answer: if God is absolute unity with no internal differentiation, how is love an eternal divine attribute? Love requires an object. Before creation, the God of Islam had no one to love — meaning love is not eternal in Allah but conditional on having created beings. The Trinity resolves this: the Father has eternally loved the Son in the fellowship of the Spirit.
1 John 4:8 does not say God loves — it says God IS love. That requires eternal, internal relationship within the Godhead.
anchor_verse: Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.

challenge: Jesus was just a prophet — a great one, but only a man
one_liner: Jesus accepted worship, forgave sins, and claimed divine identity. A mere prophet who did these things would be a blasphemer, not a model.
points: Jesus accepted worship from Thomas ('My Lord and my God,' John 20:28) and from the disciples after the resurrection (Matthew 28:9) — something no faithful Jewish prophet ever permitted.

Jesus forgave sins directly — 'Your sins are forgiven' — which his critics rightly identified as a claim to divine prerogative: 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?' (Mark 2:7). Jesus did not correct them; he confirmed the implication.
Jesus applied the divine Name to himself: 'Before Abraham was, I AM' (John 8:58) — the precise formulation of Exodus 3:14. His listeners understood the claim and took up stones.

The Quran itself calls Jesus the Messiah 11 times — yet provides no explanation of what Messiah means. In the Hebrew prophetic tradition, Messiah is the anointed deliverer who redeems through suffering and vindication (Isaiah 53). Islam uses the title while amputating its meaning.

C.S. Lewis's trilemma applies: Jesus claimed to be God. Either he was telling the truth (Lord), or he was a deliberate deceiver (Liar), or he was deluded (Lunatic). 'Great moral teacher' is not on the list of options his own words allow.
anchor_verse: I and the Father are one.

challenge: Muhammad is the Comforter (Paraclete) Jesus promised in John 14
one_liner: The Paraclete is explicitly identified as the Holy Spirit, who would dwell IN believers — not a prophet arriving 600 years later.
points: Jesus identifies the Paraclete in the same passage: 'the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name' (John 14:26). The referent is explicit and leaves no ambiguity.
The Paraclete 'dwells with you and will be in you' (John 14:17) — an indwelling presence, not a future prophet arriving in Arabia. Muhammad was not in the disciples.

The Greek word is Paraclete (Parakletos), meaning Advocate or Helper. The Islamic claim requires it to be Periklutos (meaning 'praised one' — a translation of Ahmad/Muhammad). There is no manuscript — among 5,800+ Greek manuscripts — that reads Periklutos. The substitution has no textual basis.

The Paraclete is described as 'the Spirit of truth' who 'will guide you into all truth' and 'will not speak on his own authority.' This describes the Spirit's ministry as illuminating and applying what Christ already accomplished — not initiating a new revelation superseding it.
Jesus says 'I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper' (John 14:16) — 'another of the same kind.' The Paraclete is the same kind of presence as Jesus. Muhammad was not.
anchor_verse: But the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things.

challenge: You cannot be sure of salvation — only God knows
one_liner: Christian assurance is not presumption — it is grounded in what Christ accomplished, not in our performance. Islam offers no mediator and therefore no assurance.
points: Islamic salvation is fundamentally transactional: deeds weighed against sins, with Allah's mercy as an additional but unpredictable factor. Even Muhammad said he could not guarantee his own Paradise (Sahih Bukhari 5.58.266).

Christian assurance is not based on the believer's performance but on the covenant-keeping faithfulness of God and the finished work of Christ. 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish' (John 10:28).

Justification by faith means the believer's standing before God is Christ's perfect righteousness imputed to them — not their own accumulated merit. This makes assurance possible because Christ's record does not fluctuate.
1 John 5:13 is explicit: 'I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life.' The purpose of the letter is epistemic certainty.

The question to ask: what kind of God requires perfect performance from imperfect people with no assurance of acceptance? The God who sent his own Son to bear the penalty is the God who can offer certainty.
anchor_verse: I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.

challenge: The Quran's literary beauty proves it is from God
one_liner: Literary excellence cannot verify divine origin — it is unfalsifiable and culturally conditioned. Christian verification is categorically different: a publicly witnessed, historically datable resurrection.
points: The i'jaz (inimitability) argument is circular: the Quran is miraculous because no one has matched it; no one has matched it because the Quran defines what a match looks like. The standard is set by the judge.

Pre-Islamic Arabic poetry — the Mu'allaqat, the odes of Imru' al-Qays — achieves comparable literary heights by the assessment of non-Muslim Arabic scholars. Literary greatness is real but not uniquely supernatural.
Divine origin cannot be verified by aesthetic excellence, which is always culturally and subjectively conditioned. Many religious texts are considered sublimely beautiful by their adherents.

Muhammad acknowledged he was not given public miracles like prior prophets (Sahih Bukhari 9.92.379). By his own admission, the Quran is his sole credential — but this is a private, unverifiable claim.

The Christian verification is categorically different: not 'this text is beautiful therefore divine,' but 'this man was publicly executed and publicly raised from the dead before hostile witnesses in a historically datable event.' These are different kinds of evidence, and only one is externally testable.
anchor_verse: We did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.

challenge: All religions are basically the same — just different paths to God
one_liner: Islam and Christianity make directly contradictory historical claims. They cannot both be true. Contradictory roads do not lead to the same destination.
points: Islam explicitly states Jesus was not crucified (Surah 4:157). Christianity states he was. These are direct contradictions about a historical event — not different perspectives on mystery.
Islam says Jesus was a prophet but not divine. Christianity says he is the eternal Son of God incarnate. These are not complementary views — one must be false.

The claim that all religions teach the same thing is demonstrably false on examination. Islam denies original sin, atonement, the Trinity, the Incarnation, the Resurrection, and salvation by grace — every distinctive doctrine of Christianity.
G.K. Chesterton: 'Thinking that all religions say the same thing is like thinking that all drinks are the same because they are all liquid.'

If all paths lead to God equally, then the specific claims Jesus made about himself — 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6) — are false. This cannot be reconciled with calling him a good teacher.
anchor_verse: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'

challenge: God cannot have a son — that implies physical generation (Surah 6:101)
one_liner: The Son of God is not biological offspring — it describes an eternal, metaphysical relationship of origin within the Godhead. The Quran attacks a category error no Christian theologian ever held.
points: No Christian theologian — in any century, any tradition — has ever taught that the Son of God was physically begotten. The Nicene Creed (325 AD) explicitly states 'begotten, not made' and 'eternally begotten of the Father' — a metaphysical relationship, not a biological one.

The title 'Son of God' in Hebrew scripture (Psalm 2, 2 Samuel 7) denotes a special covenantal relationship and divine appointment — not physical descent. The theological development in the New Testament maintains this covenantal logic.

John 1:1 establishes the framework: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' The Logos (Word/Son) is eternal, uncreated, and fully divine — not a created being at any point.

The Surah 6:101 argument ('How can He have a son when He has no wife?') applies human biological categories to a divine metaphysical relationship. This is a category error the Christian tradition had already identified and refuted before Islam existed.

The real question: if God cannot have an eternal Son, can God have an eternal Word? The Quran affirms the Quran is the uncreated, eternal Word of God. Christianity simply identifies that eternal Word as a Person — the Logos of John 1.
anchor_verse: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

challenge: Islam is the fastest-growing religion — doesn't that prove it's true?
one_liner: Growth rate proves sociological momentum, not theological truth. The argument would equally validate any ideology that grows rapidly.
points: This is a logical fallacy — appeal to popularity (argumentum ad populum). The number of people who hold a belief has no bearing on whether the belief is true.
By the same logic, atheism and secular humanism were the fastest-growing positions in the West for decades — which would prove neither God nor Islam by this standard.

Islam's growth is substantially demographic (high birth rates in Muslim-majority countries) rather than primarily conversion-based. Demographic growth is a sociological fact, not a theological argument.

The early Christian church grew explosively in its first three centuries while being actively persecuted by the Roman Empire — with no political or demographic advantages. Growth under persecution is a stronger argument for a movement's credibility than growth with state support.

Truth claims must be evaluated on their evidence, not their market share. The Resurrection either happened or it did not. That question is answered by historical investigation, not population statistics.
anchor_verse: Enter by the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and those who enter by it are many.

challenge: Jesus did not really rise from the dead — the disciples were mistaken or lying
one_liner: Five minimal facts accepted by the majority of critical historians — including non-Christian scholars — are best explained by a bodily resurrection, not by hallucination or deception.
points: The five minimal facts (Gary Habermas): Jesus died by crucifixion; was buried in a known tomb; the tomb was found empty; multiple individuals and groups had post-resurrection appearances; the disciples were so transformed they died for this claim.

The hallucination theory cannot explain the empty tomb or group appearances. Hallucinations are private, individual experiences — Paul reports an appearance to 500 people simultaneously (1 Corinthians 15:6).
The criterion of enemy attestation: Jewish and Roman authorities did not deny the empty tomb — they explained it as body theft (Matthew 28:13). You do not explain what does not exist.

The criterion of embarrassment: the primary witnesses in all four Gospels are women — who were not credible legal witnesses in first-century Jewish culture. An invented account would not use women as the primary witnesses.

People die for things they believe to be true but are false. Nobody dies for something they know is a lie. The disciples were in a position to know whether the resurrection occurred — and they died for it.
anchor_verse: And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins.

challenge: Christianity was changed by the Council of Nicaea — the Trinity was invented in 325 AD
one_liner: Nicaea did not invent the Trinity — it articulated in precise philosophical language what the apostolic data already required. The council clarified; it did not create.
points: The council was called because of Arius, who claimed Jesus was a created being. Nicaea ruled against Arius — which means the pre-Nicene church's majority position was already Trinitarian enough to recognize Arianism as a departure.
Pre-Nicene writers — Ignatius (c. 107 AD), Justin Martyr (c. 150 AD), Irenaeus (c. 180 AD), Tertullian (c. 200 AD) — all affirm the divinity of Christ before any council formalized the language.
John 1:1, Colossians 2:9, and Philippians 2:6 — all written in the first century — present a Christ who is fully divine. Nicaea formalized the grammar of what Scripture already stated.
The 'invented at Nicaea' claim is popularized by fiction (The Da Vinci Code) but rejected by every serious historian of early Christianity, including non-Christian scholars.
The council vote was not close: of ~300 bishops present, only 2 refused to sign. The near-unanimity reflects how deeply the pre-Nicene church already held a high Christology.
anchor_verse: For in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily.

challenge: Islam is more rational than Christianity — no mysteries like the Trinity
one_liner: Simplicity is not the same as truth. A theology that reduces God to pure undifferentiated will produces a God who cannot eternally love and whose commands are ultimately arbitrary.
points: The Trinity is not irrational — it is transrational. It addresses questions that Islamic tawhid cannot: How is love an eternal divine attribute if God was alone before creation? How can God be personal if he has no internal relational structure?

Islamic divine voluntarism — the doctrine that Allah's will determines what is good rather than his nature expressing what goodness is — produces a God whose commands are not anchored in his eternal character. Al-Ghazali stated that Allah could have destined all humanity to hell and it would be just, because justice is whatever Allah does.

The Christian God's commands are not arbitrary because they flow from his nature — he cannot lie because he IS truth; he cannot be cruel because he IS love. This grounds morality in necessity rather than sovereignty.

The 'no mysteries' claim about Islam is false on its own terms. Islamic theology contains deep mysteries: how can an eternal, unchanging God create a temporal world? How does divine decree relate to human freedom? These questions are as difficult in Islam as the Trinity is in Christianity.

C.S. Lewis: 'If Christianity was something we were making up, of course we could make it easier. But it is not. We cannot compete, in simplicity, with people who are inventing religions. How could we? We are dealing with Fact.'
anchor_verse: Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his ways!

challenge: The God of the Old Testament is violent and cruel — not a God of love
one_liner: The OT must be read in its covenantal and canonical context. Every act of judgment must be measured against the full revelation of a God who sent his own Son to bear the penalty he could have imposed on us.
points: The OT must be read within its redemptive-historical framework. The covenant with Israel was unique, temporal, and typological — pointing toward the ultimate sacrifice. The commands given to Israel in Canaan are not universal moral templates.

The God who commanded judgment in the OT is the same God who stayed Abraham's hand (Genesis 22), who pleads with Israel through the prophets, who in Ezekiel 33:11 says 'I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked.'

The cross is the interpretive key: the most violent act in the Bible is God submitting his own Son to execution on behalf of those who deserve judgment. The God who appears severe in the OT turns out to be the God who absorbed the full weight of his own justice.

Islamic theology has no equivalent problem to resolve — it simply asserts that whatever Allah commands is just by definition. The Christian must grapple honestly with OT violence; the Muslim must grapple with a God whose goodness is entirely definitional.
N.T. Wright: The cross does not reveal a God who changed his mind about violence. It reveals a God who always intended to deal with evil by taking it into himself.
anchor_verse: In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins.

challenge: Muhammad is the seal of the prophets — after him, there is no further revelation
one_liner: The New Testament was completed before Muhammad was born. If the revelation Christians hold is true, the Seal of the Prophets already came — and his name is Jesus.
points: Hebrews 1:1–2 states: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The Son is the final and definitive word — not because no more prophets are permitted, but because no fuller revelation is possible.

The claim that Muhammad is the 'seal' requires that Jesus, Paul, and John were all inferior previews of a more complete revelation. But Christianity does not present Jesus as a preview — it presents him as the substance of which every prior revelation was the shadow (Colossians 2:17).
If the resurrection of Jesus is historically verified, then the one who rose from the dead — defeating sin and death — is necessarily a greater authority than a prophet who died and remained dead.

Muhammad's own hadith record his uncertainty about his prophetic credentials upon receiving his first revelation (he feared he was possessed by a jinn, per Sahih Bukhari 1.1.3). His wife Khadijah and her cousin Waraqah — a Nestorian Christian — provided the reassurance. His initial self-authentication relied on a Christian.

Deuteronomy 18:21–22 provides the prophet test: if a prophecy does not come true, the prophet did not speak for God. Muhammad made specific predictions that failed or required revision. Jesus predicted his own death and resurrection — and rose.
anchor_verse: But in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed the heir of all things.

challenge: Christians changed the Bible to make Jesus look like God
one_liner: The earliest manuscripts we have — predating any alleged conspiracy — already contain the high Christology Islam claims was added later. There is no earlier, lower-Christology manuscript tradition.
points: The Rylands Papyrus (P52) — the earliest NT fragment, dated c. 117–138 AD — contains John 18:31–33, 37–38. The Gospel of John, which contains the highest Christology ('In the beginning was the Word'), is attested within living memory of the apostolic generation.

Paul's letter to the Philippians (c. 62 AD) contains the Christ hymn of Philippians 2:6–11 — describing Christ as 'in the form of God' — within 30 years of the crucifixion. There is no earlier Pauline text with a lower Christology.

The 'corruption' theory requires coordinating thousands of manuscripts across multiple continents and languages — Greek, Latin, Syriac, Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian — simultaneously and perfectly, leaving no dissenting manuscript anywhere. No historian accepts this.

Non-Christian sources confirm the early high Christology: Pliny the Younger (c. 112 AD) reports that Christians 'sing hymns to Christ as to a god.' This is the secular Roman record, 200 years before Nicaea.

The burden of proof lies with those who claim corruption: produce a manuscript with a lower Christology, or a historical account of the corruption occurring. After 1,400 years, neither has been produced.
anchor_verse: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth.

challenge: Jesus never claimed to be God — that is a later Christian invention
one_liner: Jesus made divine claims throughout the Gospels — which is precisely why the Jewish authorities charged him with blasphemy and crucified him. His enemies understood what his followers sometimes missed.
points: John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I AM.' This is the direct appropriation of the divine Name from Exodus 3:14. His listeners understood it as such and immediately took up stones to stone him for blasphemy.

Mark 2:5–7: Jesus says 'Your sins are forgiven.' His critics respond: 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?' Jesus does not correct them — he confirms the implication by healing the paralytic as proof of his authority.
John 10:30: 'I and the Father are one.' Again, his hearers took up stones — not because they misunderstood him, but because they understood him perfectly.

Matthew 26:63–65: Before the high priest, Jesus is asked under oath whether he is the Son of God. He answers: 'You have said so. But I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven.' The priest tears his robes and charges blasphemy. The charge requires a divine claim — not a prophetic one.

The charge of 'later invention' must explain why every strand of the New Testament tradition — Paul, Mark, Matthew, Luke, John — independently presents a divine Christ, and why the earliest non-Christian sources confirm Christians were worshipping Christ as God before any council met.
anchor_verse: Jesus said to them, 'Truly, truly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I am.'

challenge: Science has disproved religion — we do not need God to explain the universe
one_liner: Science explains how the universe operates. It cannot answer why anything exists at all, why the laws of physics are what they are, or why there is something rather than nothing.
points: Science operates within the natural order — it describes regularities in how nature behaves. The question of why there is a natural order to describe at all lies outside science's domain.

The fine-tuning of the universe — constants calibrated to life-permitting precision at odds of 1 in 10^(10^123) by Roger Penrose's calculation — is a scientific discovery that points beyond science for its explanation.

The origin of the universe (the Big Bang, confirmed by the BGV theorem) establishes a cosmic beginning — which science can describe but not explain. Causes precede effects; what caused the cause of everything cannot itself be part of the causal series.

Thomas Nagel — an atheist philosopher — wrote Mind and Cosmos (2012) arguing that consciousness, reason, and value cannot be explained by a purely materialist account of nature. The problem is not religion versus science but materialism versus reality.

Every scientist uses logic, mathematics, and the assumption that the universe is rationally ordered and knowable. These presuppositions are not themselves scientific findings — they are conditions that make science possible. Christianity provides the metaphysical grounding for why nature should be rational and knowable at all.
anchor_verse: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

challenge: If God exists and is good, why is there so much suffering?
one_liner: The problem of evil is real and must be taken seriously — but it is a problem every worldview must answer. Christianity is the only worldview whose God entered the suffering himself.
points: The logical problem of evil (the claim that God's existence and evil's existence are logically incompatible) has been philosophically defeated. Alvin Plantinga's free will defense demonstrates that an omnipotent God and genuinely free creatures can coexist — and free creatures generate moral evil.

The evidential problem of evil (the claim that the amount of suffering makes God's existence improbable) requires the critic to know that no sufficient reason for God permitting suffering exists — which requires exhaustive knowledge the critic does not have.

The cross is Christianity's answer: God did not watch suffering from a distance and offer explanations. He entered it. The eternal Son of God suffered the worst the world could inflict — and redeemed it.

Islam has no equivalent answer. Allah is entirely transcendent — he does not suffer with his creation. The God of Islam permits suffering from infinite distance; the God of the Bible absorbs it in the person of his Son.

The moral outrage behind the problem of evil borrows from Christian premises: suffering is wrong, the innocent should not suffer, something ought to be different. These intuitions require an objective moral standard — which only theism can ground. The problem of evil is a problem that only makes sense inside a framework that requires God to solve it.
anchor_verse: He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.

challenge: Islam's five daily prayers show greater devotion and discipline than Christianity
one_liner: Frequency of ritual is not the same as relationship. Christianity does not replace prayer with proximity — it offers unmediated access to God through the Son, available at all times, not five prescribed moments.
points: The five daily prayers (salat) are a beautiful discipline of remembrance. But in Islamic theology, they are primarily acts of submission (the word Islam means submission) — the creature acknowledging the Creator's sovereignty.

Christianity offers something categorically different: not prescribed ritual access but adoptive sonship. 'You have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry, Abba! Father!' (Romans 8:15). This is not the language of a subject petitioning a sovereign — it is the language of a child addressing a Father.

The Temple veil torn at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51) signifies permanent, unmediated access to God's presence — available to all believers at all times, not only at prescribed hours or facing a designated direction.

In Islamic theology, Allah does not have a personal relationship with believers in the way the New Testament describes. He is merciful and compassionate — but the believer cannot know they are loved as a child, only that they are servant to a Master.
The question is not which religion produces more visible religious activity. The question is which God is actually knowable, and on what terms.
anchor_verse: For you did not receive the spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry, 'Abba! Father!'
